text.title: Spatial Accessibility Rules and Input Modality Requirements
text.summary: Mandatory rules for accessible spatial UI: every interaction needs two input modalities, a non-spatial fallback, and seated-mode parity; comfort and caption requirements are enumerated.

rule.statement: Every spatial interaction must support at least two independent input modalities; one must not be gaze-and-dwell if hand tracking is the primary.
rule.rationale: Motor limitations, gloves, controllers-only environments, and hand-tracking failures all eliminate single-modality paths. Two independent modalities ensure at least one remains viable.

rule.statement: Provide a non-spatial fallback (flat-window mode, companion app, or voice-only path) for every critical user flow.
rule.rationale: Users unable to tolerate immersion or lacking the physical space for room-scale interaction cannot be excluded from core functionality.

example.note: Select object: gaze-and-pinch (primary), voice command "select" (secondary), controller trigger (tertiary). Flat-window fallback for seated users without hand tracking.
Select object: grab gesture only. No alternative; users with motor impairment or low-FOV devices are locked out.

rule.statement: Every interaction that works standing must also work from a seated position; document any exception with an explicit accommodation alternative.
rule.rationale: Enterprise XR, rehabilitation contexts, and users with mobility impairments require seated-mode parity. "Designed for standing" without a seated path excludes a legally-protected population in regulated industries.

rule.statement: Reposition UI to seated eye-height (approximately 1.0-1.2 m above ground) when seated mode is active; do not require the user to look up beyond 20° above horizontal.
rule.rationale: Sustained upward head tilt causes neck fatigue within minutes; ergonomic guidelines for standing-height UI are not appropriate in seated contexts.

section.title: Comfort and Captions

rule.statement: Offer both vignette and teleport locomotion; never force a single comfort solution — comfort is individually variable.
rule.rationale: Motion sickness triggers differ per user. Smooth locomotion causes nausea in some; snap-turn triggers others. Configurable options are the only reliable accommodation.

rule.statement: Default spatial captions to head-locked subtitles at 3 m apparent distance; provide a world-locked option with size and contrast controls.
rule.rationale: Head-locked captions are readable in motion but break immersion. World-locked captions are immersive but unreadable during fast motion. Defaults favour readability; override favours immersion.

rule.statement: Provide audio descriptions for all cinematic and interactive XR moments that convey meaning through visuals alone.
rule.rationale: VoiceOver on visionOS covers only partial screen content; hand-authored audio descriptions remain required for blind users in immersive contexts.

section.reference: TypeScript auditor: checks each interaction has minimum 2 input modalities
